title: Licensed Practical Nurse
metaTitle: Licensed Practical Nurse Resume: Tips & Examples
metaDescription: Build a strong LPN resume with real examples, key skills, and ATS keywords. Land nursing interviews faster.
h1: Licensed Practical Nurse Resume: What Hiring Managers Want to See
intro: Your LPN resume needs to show hands-on patient care skills and clinical experience in a way that gets past both ATS systems and nursing recruiters. We'll walk you through the exact format, skills, and bullets that land interviews at hospitals, clinics, and long-term care facilities.
audience: New LPN graduates applying for first jobs, experienced LPNs switching specialties or facilities, and career-changers with nursing credentials.

topSkills.name: Patient Care & Assessment
topSkills.why: Recruiters need proof you can safely monitor vitals, recognize patient changes, and document findings — the core of LPN work.

topSkills.name: Electronic Health Records (EHR)
topSkills.why: Nearly every facility uses EHR systems (Epic, Cerner, etc.); employers want to know you're comfortable with digital charting.

topSkills.name: Medication Administration & IV Therapy
topSkills.why: State-specific, but LPNs who can safely administer meds and manage IV lines are in higher demand and earn more.

topSkills.name: Wound Care & Dressing Changes
topSkills.why: A concrete clinical skill that appears frequently in job postings and separates experienced LPNs from entry-level candidates.

topSkills.name: Catheterization & Foley Care
topSkills.why: Urinary catheter management is a routine LPN task; demonstrating comfort here signals readiness for most patient floors.

topSkills.name: Vital Signs Monitoring
topSkills.why: Basic but essential; employers want to see you can identify abnormal readings and escalate appropriately.

topSkills.name: HIPAA Compliance & Patient Confidentiality
topSkills.why: Healthcare employers screen hard for this; showing you understand privacy rules builds trust immediately.

topSkills.name: Team Collaboration & Communication
topSkills.why: Nursing is a team sport; recruiters look for LPNs who document clearly, report changes, and work well with RNs and aides.

topSkills.name: Patient Education
topSkills.why: Demonstrating you can teach patients about medications, discharge care, or disease prevention shows initiative beyond task completion.

sampleBullets.weak: Provided patient care and monitored vitals on a busy medical-surgical unit.
sampleBullets.strong: Monitored and recorded vital signs for 8-12 patients per shift on medical-surgical unit; escalated 3+ abnormal readings weekly to RN, preventing 2 readmissions in Q3.
sampleBullets.lesson: Specific patient load, quantified outcomes, and demonstrated clinical judgment turn a generic duty into proof of impact.

sampleBullets.weak: Assisted with wound care and dressing changes as assigned.
sampleBullets.strong: Performed sterile dressing changes on 15-20 surgical and pressure wounds per week; documented wound assessment findings (size, color, drainage) in EHR; maintained 0 infections in assigned patients over 6-month period.
sampleBullets.lesson: Numbers (frequency, volume, outcomes) and specific EHR documentation skills show competence and consistency.

sampleBullets.weak: Collaborated with nursing team to deliver quality patient care.
sampleBullets.strong: Communicated patient status changes to RN charge nurse within 5 minutes of observation; coordinated with CNAs on toileting and hygiene; received 'excellent teamwork' on 4 consecutive quarterly reviews.
sampleBullets.lesson: Concrete actions (timing, coordination, feedback) replace vague 'collaboration' with proof of reliable partnership.

commonMistakes.fix: Replace 'provided care to patients' with measurable outcomes: number of patients, specific skills used, complication rates, or feedback received.

commonMistakes.mistake: Omitting EHR and clinical system names
commonMistakes.fix: Name the exact systems you've used (Epic, Cerner, Meditech) — recruiters and ATS systems search for these as proof of technical readiness.

commonMistakes.mistake: Skipping state licensure or certification details
commonMistakes.fix: Clearly list your LPN license number/state, BLS/CPR expiration date, and any specialty certifications (e.g., IV therapy, phlebotomy) in a dedicated section near the top.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using generic healthcare buzzwords without context
commonMistakes.fix: Instead of 'compassionate care,' show it: 'Spent 10 minutes educating post-op patient on pain management, reducing call light use by 40% during recovery.'

commonMistakes.mistake: Burying clinical experience behind soft skills
commonMistakes.fix: Lead your experience section with clinical skills and patient outcomes; save teamwork and communication for the bullet explanation.

structureTips: Put your LPN license and BLS/CPR credentials at the very top (right below your name) — recruiters scan for this first and many use it as an auto-filter.
Lead your Professional Experience section with your most recent clinical role and quantify patient load, shift type (nights/days), and unit specialty (ICU, med-surg, long-term care).
Create a separate 'Clinical Skills' section listing EHR systems, medication prep, wound care, catheterization, and any specialty training (IV therapy, phlebotomy, etc.) — this is ATS gold.
If you're early-career, include your nursing school clinical rotations with specific unit placements and supervisor contact info; hiring managers may verify.
salarySignal: Entry-level LPN salaries in the US typically range from $35,000–$42,000 annually; experienced LPNs in high-demand settings (ICU, home health) often earn $45,000–$55,000+, depending on location and shift differentials.

faq.q: Should I list my nursing school clinical rotations on an LPN resume if I have no job experience yet?
faq.a: Yes. New grads should dedicate a 'Clinical Experience' or 'Practicum' section to your nursing school rotations, listing the unit, facility, patient populations, and key skills practiced. Include your supervisor's name and contact info if possible — employers may verify. Then add any CNA or medical assistant work in a separate 'Work Experience' section.

faq.q: What's the best way to format my LPN license and BLS/CPR info on my resume?
faq.a: Create a 'Licensure & Certifications' section immediately below your contact info or at the top of your resume. List: 'Licensed Practical Nurse, [State], License #[number], Expires [date]' and 'BLS/CPR Certification, American Heart Association, Expires [date].' This is non-negotiable — recruiters filter on it.

faq.q: How many patients should I mention on my resume if I worked night shifts with smaller patient loads?
faq.a: Be honest about your actual typical load. If you cared for 6–8 patients on nights, say so; if you floated between units and the load varied, write 'varied patient assignments ranging from 5–12 patients depending on unit acuity.' Context matters more than inflated numbers.

faq.q: Should I list every EHR system I've touched, or just the major ones?
faq.a: List every system you've actually used hands-on (Epic, Cerner, Meditech, etc.) — ATS systems search for these exact names. If you're only familiar with one, list that one with confidence. Don't claim proficiency in systems you've never used.

faq.q: How do I write about infection control or safety achievements as an LPN?
faq.a: Use concrete metrics: 'Maintained zero HAIs (hospital-acquired infections) among assigned patients over 12-month period' or 'Completed quarterly safety audits with 98% compliance on hand hygiene protocols.' These show employers you take safety seriously and have measurable track records.
